facility_name: Highlander Hainan Underwater Intelligent Computing Center
full_address: South China Sea, off Lingshui County, Hainan Island, China
customer_type_if_known: China Telecom, Tencent, SenseTime, 7+ enterprise clients
build_phase_if_known: 2 capsules operational (Dec 2022 + Feb 2025). Expansion to 100 capsules planned within 5 years.
cooling_type: Passive seawater cooling -- ocean acts as free heat exchanger. No chillers or pumps required. Steel capsule coated with glass flakes for corrosion resistance.
opportunity_summary: World's first commercially operational underwater data center cluster. Two 1,300-tonne capsules submerged 35m underwater off Hainan Island use the South China Sea as a passive cooling system. Each capsule holds 24 server racks (up to 500 servers). Combined cluster has ~1,200 servers capable of handling 7,000 DeepSeek conversations per second. PUE 1.1 -- 30% better than China's national average of 1.5. Expansion to 100 capsules across 68,000 sqm of seabed planned within 5 years. Saves 90% of cooling energy vs. traditional DCs.
confidence_reason: Multiple primary sources: DCD, SCMP, Xinhua, Merics, China Daily all confirm operational status, capsule specs, and PUE. Second capsule installation confirmed February 2025. DeepSeek performance benchmarks from SCMP report.
source_1_title: China's underwater data center expanded
source_1_evidence_snippet: Second capsule installed February 2025, expanding total server capacity. New capsule 18 meters in length, designed for high-performance and AI workloads.
source_2_title: China's subsea data centre could power 7,000 DeepSeek conversations a second
source_2_evidence_snippet: Expanded undersea DC handles 7,000 DeepSeek conversations per second. Computing power equivalent to 30,000 high-end gaming PCs.
notes: UNDERWATER / SUBSEA. First commercial underwater DC in the world. Operated jointly by Highlander and China Telecom. Each capsule weighs 1,300 tonnes, submerged 35m. Glass-flake coated steel for corrosion resistance. 90% cooling energy savings. 40-60% more power efficient than traditional facilities. Clients include Tencent and SenseTime for AI workloads.
current_phase: 2 capsules operational, scaling to 100 capsules
expansion_details: Plan to grow to 100 capsules within 5 years, forming a large subsea campus across 68,000 sqm of seabed near the coast.
pipeline_evidence_snippet: Highlander completes first commercial underwater data center, looks for exports. Plans to grow cluster to 100 capsules within five years.

facility_name: HiCloud Shanghai Lingang Wind-Powered Underwater Data Center
full_address: Offshore, Lingang Special Area, China (Shanghai) Pilot Free Trade Zone
build_phase_if_known: Phase 1: 2.3MW demonstration facility, expected operational Sept 2025. Phase 2: scale to 24MW.
cooling_type: Seawater cooling via modular data units submerged underwater. Pressure-resistant steel housing with circulating seawater heat exchangers. Reduces refrigeration energy from 40-50% to under 10% of total power.
opportunity_summary: World's first offshore wind-powered underwater data center. Phase 1 is a 2.3MW demonstration facility (198 server racks) designated by China's NDRC as a national model for green, low-carbon innovation. Phase 2 scales to 24MW with PUE below 1.15. Over 90% power from offshore wind farms. Initial investment of 1.6 billion yuan ($222.8M). Reduces overall energy use by 30-40% vs traditional DCs. Long-term vision: 500MW subsea deployment.
confidence_reason: Agreement signed June 10, 2025 between Lingang Special Area, Lingang Investment Holding, and HiCloud. Reported by Xinhua (state media), DCD, CGTN, Offshore Wind. Phase 1 completion confirmed October 2025 by Xinhua.
source_1_title: World's first wind-powered commercial underwater data center project launched in Shanghai
source_1_evidence_snippet: Tripartite cooperation agreement signed for first commercial underwater data center powered by offshore wind farm in Shanghai Lingang Special Area.
source_2_title: China's HiCloud launches wind-powered underwater data center, targets 500MW subsea deployment
source_2_evidence_snippet: Phase 1: 2.3MW, 198 racks. Phase 2: 24MW. PUE target below 1.15. 90%+ offshore wind power. HiCloud targets 500MW long-term subsea deployment.
certification_signal: NDRC national model for green low-carbon innovation
notes: UNDERWATER / OFFSHORE WIND. World's first wind-powered commercial underwater DC. 1.6B yuan initial investment. 198 server racks in Phase 1. Reduces power consumption by 22.8%, water use by 100%, and land use by 90%+ vs traditional DCs. NDRC-designated national model project. Long-term 500MW subsea vision.
current_phase: Phase 1 (2.3MW) completed Oct 2025; Phase 2 (24MW) under development
expansion_details: Phase 2 scales to 24MW. HiCloud has set sights on 500MW subsea deployment, envisioning a new model of low-carbon, high-density computing powered by sea and wind.
pipeline_evidence_snippet: China completes world's first wind-powered underwater data center. Phase 1 operational Oct 2025.

facility_name: Microsoft Project Natick -- Northern Isles (Decommissioned Research)
full_address: European Marine Energy Centre, Orkney Islands, Scotland, UK
build_phase_if_known: Phase 2 deployed June 2018, retrieved 2020, project officially ended June 2024.
cooling_type: Sealed nitrogen-filled capsule submerged 117 feet deep. Passive seawater cooling of sealed vessel. No active cooling plant required. Nitrogen atmosphere reduced corrosion and server failures.
opportunity_summary: Pioneering underwater data center research project. Phase 2 capsule (12m long, 2m diameter) held 864 servers across 12 racks at 20kW/rack. Submerged at 117 feet in Orkney for 2 years. Key finding: 1/8th the server failure rate of land-based equivalents. Nitrogen atmosphere and absence of human activity identified as primary factors. Project officially ended June 2024 -- underwater pods cannot be easily upgraded for AI GPU workloads. Lessons now applied to Microsoft's land-based liquid immersion cooling strategy.
confidence_reason: Microsoft primary source (news.microsoft.com, natick.research.microsoft.com). DCD and Tom's Hardware confirmed project end in June 2024. Server failure rate and specifications from Microsoft's own published research results.
source_1_title: Microsoft finds underwater datacenters are reliable, practical and use energy sustainably
source_1_evidence_snippet: Servers in Natick Northern Isles showed a failure rate of 1/8th that of land-based control group. Nitrogen atmosphere and absence of people identified as primary reasons.
source_2_title: Microsoft shelves its underwater data center -- Project Natick had fewer server failures compared to servers on land
source_2_evidence_snippet: Microsoft officially shelved Project Natick in June 2024. Underwater pods remain sealed for years, making GPU upgrades impractical for AI workloads.
notes: UNDERWATER / DECOMMISSIONED RESEARCH. Pioneered underwater DC concept. Phase 1 (2015): small capsule off California coast. Phase 2 (2018): 864-server capsule off Orkney, Scotland at EMEC tidal energy site. Key result: 8x fewer server failures than land. Ended because sealed pods cannot accommodate GPU upgrades needed for AI era. Microsoft now applies lessons to land-based immersion cooling. Naval Group (France) built the capsule.
current_phase: Project ended June 2024. No underwater DCs currently operational by Microsoft.
expansion_details: Project terminated. Lessons applied to land-based liquid immersion cooling research.
pipeline_evidence_snippet: Microsoft confirms Project Natick underwater data center is no more. No underwater DCs currently in operation.

facility_name: Subsea Cloud Jules Verne Pod -- Port Angeles
full_address: Offshore, Port Angeles, Washington, USA
customer_type_if_known: AI companies, gaming industry (per company claims)
build_phase_if_known: Jules Verne pod deployed. Njord01 (Gulf of Mexico) and Manannan (North Sea) planned.
cooling_type: Pressure-equalized immersion cooling. Interior matches external water pressure (no thick walls needed). Servers sit in dielectric immersion fluid inside sealed chamber. Heat dissipated to surrounding seawater. No chillers or cooling towers.
opportunity_summary: Pressure-equalized underwater data center pod submerged ~9m near Port Angeles, WA. 20-foot container-sized pod with 16 server racks (~800 servers) delivering ~1MW IT capacity. Connected to shore via 100 Gbps fiber. Unique pressure-equalized design eliminates structural stress. Servers in dielectric immersion fluid. Claimed PUE of 0.88-1.01 (effectively no cooling overhead). 40% CO2 reduction vs traditional DCs. Two additional deployments planned: Njord01 (Gulf of Mexico) and Manannan (North Sea, 200-300m depth).
confidence_reason: Company website and DCD report details. Hardware completion confirmed mid-2023. PUE claims are vendor-reported and exceptionally low (0.88) -- not independently verified. Actual commercial workload status unclear. Two planned deployments (Njord01, Manannan) not yet confirmed operational.
source_1_title: Subsea Cloud -- Official Website
source_1_evidence_snippet: Pressure-equalized pods. Interior matches external water pressure. Servers in dielectric immersion fluid. PUE 0.88-1.01. 40% CO2 reduction.
source_2_title: Subsea Cloud announces three underwater data center projects
source_2_evidence_snippet: Jules Verne (Port Angeles), Njord01 (Gulf of Mexico), Manannan (North Sea, 200-300m depth). 16 racks, ~800 servers, ~1MW per pod.
notes: UNDERWATER / IMMERSION. Pressure-equalized design is unique -- no sealed pressure vessel. Dielectric immersion fluid inside pods. Servers never contact seawater. 100 Gbps fiber to shore. Operations in USA, UK, Norway. Company also claims 13,500 servers in Asia -- not independently verified. Demo/compliance unit open to regulator inspection.
current_phase: Jules Verne pod operational. Njord01 and Manannan in planning.
expansion_details: Njord01 planned for Gulf of Mexico. Manannan planned for North Sea at 200-300m depth.
pipeline_evidence_snippet: Three underwater data center projects announced: Jules Verne (Port Angeles), Njord01 (Gulf of Mexico), Manannan (North Sea).

facility_name: NetworkOcean Underwater Data Center -- San Francisco Bay (Proposed)
full_address: San Francisco Bay, California, USA (proposed)
build_phase_if_known: 0.5MW capsule in development. Permitting challenges unresolved. Pivoted to floating approach.
cooling_type: Seawater/ocean cooling for submerged GPU servers. Claims 30% energy reduction. Specific cooling architecture not publicly detailed.
opportunity_summary: Y Combinator-backed startup (Summer 2024 batch) attempting to build underwater GPU data centers. Claims 2,048 Nvidia H100 GPUs available at $2.10/GPU/hr. 0.5MW capsule under development for San Francisco Bay test. Ran into major regulatory issues -- BCDC and SF Regional Water Quality Control Board warned of fines for unpermitted deployment. Now experimenting with floating server approach. ~$1M raised. High ambition, early stage, regulatory risk.
confidence_reason: YC company page and DCD article confirm existence and plans. However, significant regulatory challenges unresolved. No confirmed underwater deployment. GPU availability claims not independently verified. Pivot to floating approach suggests original plan may not proceed. Very early stage.
source_1_title: NetworkOcean plans underwater data center test in San Francisco Bay
source_1_evidence_snippet: NetworkOcean plans to sink a 500kW data center capsule into San Francisco Bay. Backed by YC. Claims 2,048 H100 GPUs. Regulators not notified.
source_2_title: NetworkOcean | Y Combinator
source_2_evidence_snippet: We build and operate underwater data centers. 2,048 H100s available for reservation. Cut power usage by up to 30%.
notes: UNDERWATER / PROPOSED. YC S24 startup. Ran into regulatory issues with BCDC and SF Regional Water Quality Control Board for attempting unpermitted deployment. Scientists warned even slight heat could trigger toxic algae blooms. Pivoted to floating server concept. Very early stage, high risk.
current_phase: Capsule in development. Regulatory hurdles unresolved. Pivoting to floating approach.
expansion_details: Plans to scale ocean-based GPU infrastructure. Regulatory path unclear.
pipeline_evidence_snippet: NetworkOcean plans underwater data center test in San Francisco Bay. Faces regulatory pushback.

facility_name: DeepGreen Western Passage Tidal-Powered Underwater Data Center (Proposed)
full_address: Western Passage, off Kendall's Head, Eastport, Maine, USA
build_phase_if_known: FERC preliminary permit application submitted Feb 2026. 48-month environmental study period requested.
cooling_type: Subsea ocean cooling. AI compute pods docked in universal docking cradles on ocean floor. Seawater provides natural cooling for submerged server pods.
opportunity_summary: Proposed 51MW tidal energy + subsea AI data center in Western Passage, Maine. 170 tidal turbines + 34 AI compute pods on ocean floor using 'universal docking cradles.' $415M estimated cost. FERC preliminary permit filed Feb 2026 for 48-month environmental study. Would also supply power to Eastport residents and the Passamaquoddy Tribe. Active infrastructure occupies ~27 acres of a 400-acre permit area. Developer also exploring Cook Inlet, Alaska. Very early stage -- years from operational.
confidence_reason: FERC application is a public record (confirmed by DCD, Bangor Daily News, Press Herald). However, project is very early stage -- only a preliminary permit for studies, not construction. Developer is a real estate broker (Louis Wolfson). No proven subsea DC technology demonstrated. Environmental and permitting challenges significant.

expansion_details: Also exploring similar project at Cook Inlet, Alaska.
pipeline_evidence_snippet: FERC preliminary permit application submitted Feb 11, 2026. 170 turbines + 34 data center pods across 400 acres.

facility_name: Nautilus Data Technologies -- Stockton 1 Floating Barge Data Center
full_address: Port of Stockton, San Joaquin River, Stockton, CA, USA
customer_type_if_known: Colocation (86% leased as of late 2024). Backblaze is a known tenant.
build_phase_if_known: Stockton 1 operational since 2021. Listed for sale at $45M (Oct 2025). LA and Marseille expansions planned.
cooling_type: Patented natural water cooling system. Uses river water as outside cooling loop (closed-loop, zero water consumption). Eliminates chillers and cooling towers entirely. Supports rack densities up to 55kW+.
opportunity_summary: Pioneering floating barge data center on the San Joaquin River at Port of Stockton. 230-foot (70m) barge with 6.5MW IT capacity across 4 secure data vaults (20,000 sqft). PUE 1.15. Patented natural water cooling eliminates chillers -- zero water consumption, no chemical use, no water pollution. Supports 55kW+ per rack. 86% leased. Listed for sale at $45M in Oct 2025. Company pivoting to EcoCore modular product line and expanding to Los Angeles (AltaSea at Port of LA) and Marseille, France. Bechtel partnership for future builds.
confidence_reason: Operational since 2021. DCD confirmed sale listing at $45M. PUE and cooling specs from company website and Data Center Frontier. Backblaze confirmed as tenant. Bechtel partnership announced 2021. Series D funding Jan 2026.
source_1_title: Stockton Data Center | Nautilus Data Technologies
source_1_evidence_snippet: 6.5MW scalable critical IT load across four secure vaults. PUE 1.15. Zero-water-use natural water cooling. 55kW+ rack density.

source_2_evidence_snippet: 20,000 sqft floating data center. 86% leased. Listed at $45M. Consistent with Nautilus focus on expanding EcoCore technology platform.

Bechtel partnership for future builds. EcoCore modular product (2.5MW) launched. Series D funding Jan 2026. LA facility at AltaSea campus, Port of Los Angeles. Marseille near submarine cable landing stations.

facility_name: Kinetics-MOL Floating Data Center Platform (Under Development)
full_address: Location TBD (targeting Japan, Malaysia, and US markets)
build_phase_if_known: MoU signed July 2025. Feasibility study 2025. Construction 2026. Deployment target 2027.
cooling_type: Seawater / river water cooling via closed-loop systems on retrofitted vessel. Minimizes heated water discharge. Investigating renewable power integration (offshore wind, solar).
opportunity_summary: Joint venture between Karpowership subsidiary Kinetics and Mitsui O.S.K. Lines to develop floating data centers on retrofitted vessels. Planned vessel: 120m long, 9,731 gross tons, with modular data center architecture supporting 20-73MW IT load. Seawater closed-loop cooling. Power from Powerships (Karpowership's specialty: 10,000MW installed across 19 countries), with optional grid, solar, or offshore wind integration. 1-year conversion timeline vs 3 years for land-based DC. Targeting Japan, Malaysia, US. MoU signed July 2025.
confidence_reason: MoU confirmed by MOL press release (primary source) and DCD. Both companies are established (Karpowership: 10GW+ powerships; MOL: major Japanese shipping company). However, project is in feasibility study stage. No vessel identified yet. No cooling specs confirmed beyond general seawater closed-loop intent.

facility_name: Aikido Technologies Offshore Wind-Turbine Data Center (Prototype)
full_address: North Sea, off coast of Norway (prototype location)
build_phase_if_known: 100kW prototype with refurbished Vestas V-17 turbine. Launch target: end of 2026 in Norway North Sea.
cooling_type: Closed-loop freshwater ballast cooling. Three semi-submersible legs each house a liquid-cooled data hall. Freshwater stored in ballast system transfers heat through steel walls into surrounding North Sea water. No active chillers.
opportunity_summary: San Francisco startup building data centers inside offshore wind turbine platforms. Prototype: 100kW unit with refurbished Vestas V-17 turbine launching in Norway North Sea by end of 2026. Full-scale concept (AO60DC): semi-submersible platform with 3 ballast-filled legs, each housing a 3-4MW liquid-cooled data hall (total 9-12MW per turbine). Freshwater ballast cooling with heat dissipated to sea through steel walls. Oil-and-gas style semi-submersible design. First commercial project targeted for UK in 2028.
confidence_reason: Covered by Tom's Hardware, IEEE Spectrum, New Atlas, TechRadar, TechSpot. Detailed technical concept published. However, prototype is not yet deployed. Very early stage startup. Commercial viability unproven. First commercial project (UK) targeted 2028 -- years away.

facility_name: Green Mountain DC1-Stavanger (Former NATO Bunker, Fjord-Cooled)
full_address: Rennesoy, near Stavanger, Norway
facility_type: Underground Mountain / Former NATO Bunker Data Center
build_phase_if_known: Phase 1 operational. Expandable to 26MW.
cooling_type: Gravity-fed fjord water cooling. Cold water drawn from 75m+ depth (stable 8C year-round) in adjacent fjord. No powered chillers or refrigerant gases. Gravity-driven -- no energy required to transport coolant. Closed-loop chilled water under raised floors. Near-zero WUE (no evaporative systems).
opportunity_summary: Former NATO ammunition storage facility buried 250m inside a mountain on Rennesoy island, Norway. Three 2-storey concrete buildings within the mountain. Fjord water cooling uses gravity to deliver 8C water from 75m+ depth -- no energy for cooling transport. 100% renewable hydropower. PUE 1.2. Near-zero WUE. Carbon neutral. Expandable to 26MW. High physical security (former military bunker). Supports enterprise colocation with financial-grade SLAs.
confidence_reason: Green Mountain official website, DCD, Data Center Knowledge, ABB case study, Victaulic project page all confirm details. PUE 1.2 and fjord cooling well-documented. Former NATO bunker status confirmed by multiple sources.

current_phase: Phase 1 operational. Expandable to 26MW.
pipeline_evidence_snippet: Phase 1 expandable to 26MW.

facility_name: Lefdal Mine Datacenter (Underground Olivine Mine, Fjord-Cooled)
full_address: Between Maloy and Nordfjordeid, west coast of Norway
customer_type_if_known: HPC, AI, research (Olivia supercomputer for Sigma2/UNINETT). Enterprise colocation.

cooling_type: Fjord water cooling from 100m depth in 500m-deep fjord (stable 8C). 12 heat exchangers totaling 90MW cooling capacity. Chilled water under raised floors via closed circuit. opportunity_summary: Former olivine mine 60m below sea level and 700m inside a mountain on Norway's west coast. 12 modular heat exchangers (90MW total cooling) draw 8C water from 100m depth in a 500m-deep fjord. Supports up to 300kW+ per rack via DLC and immersion cooling (designing 400kW solutions). 100% hydro and wind power. Guaranteed PUE 1.15. Near-zero WUE. Hosts Olivia -- Norway's most powerful supercomputer (HPE Cray EX, 13.2 petaflops, 64,512 CPUs + 304 GPUs, ranked #22 Green500). 225M NOK ($22.6M) system inaugurated June 2025. Expandable to 200MW and 120,000 sqm whitespace.

facility_name: Bahnhof Pionen -- Underground Nuclear Bunker Data Center (Stockholm)
full_address: Sodermalm, Stockholm, Sweden (30m below granite in White Mountains)
build_phase_if_known: Operational since 2008. Heat recovery upgrade completed.
cooling_type: Outside air cooling leveraging Sweden's cold climate. 1.5MW Baltimore Aircoil cooling fans. Heat recovery: two heat pumps route waste heat through 70m pipe to district heating network (heats private households). Saves 275 MWh/year.
opportunity_summary: Iconic data center 30m below granite in a 1943-era nuclear bunker in Stockholm's Sodermalm district. 50cm thick steel doors. Can withstand a hydrogen bomb. 1,100 sqm. 140 cabinets at 5.7kW avg density. 21MW power capacity. Backup: two Maybach MTU diesel engines (1.5MW). Innovative heat recovery: waste heat piped to district heating network, heating private homes. Saves 275 MWh/year and E40K/year on electricity. Designed with James Bond villain aesthetic.

facility_name: InfoBunker -- Military-Grade Underground Data Center (Iowa)
full_address: Near Des Moines, Iowa, USA (50+ feet underground)
build_phase_if_known: Operational. 65,000 sqft total, 38,000 sqft DC space. 10MW capacity.
cooling_type: 3x DX cooling units, 3x economizers, 2x dry coolers in N+2 redundancy. Switched to dry cooling and economizer for 6-8 months/year. Underground location provides natural thermal advantages.
opportunity_summary: Military-grade hardened data center 50+ feet underground. 3-foot thick reinforced concrete. Designed to survive a 20-megaton nuclear blast from 2.5 miles. 10MW utility power via dedicated substation (ring-fed from 2 other substations). 3x 750kVA turbine diesel backup generators (N+2 or 2N+1). EMP shielded. 10-tonne blast doors. Dry cooling and economizer mode for 6-8 months/year. 65,000 sqft total (38,000 sqft DC). SSAE 16, SOC 1/2/3, PCI-DSS, HIPAA compliant.
